Meteora Dynamic Slippage: A Key Tool for Enhanced Liquidity Provision

Providing liquidity goes beyond simply depositing assets into a pool and waiting for fees. A liquidity provider understands that execution and market conditions are key to activating a pool.

One crucial feature enhancing this experience is Dynamic Slippage on @MeteoraAG DLMM.

Slippage refers to the difference between the anticipated price when a trader initiates a swap and the actual price received after execution.

In stable market conditions, this difference is typically minimal. However, during high volatility, prices fluctuate rapidly, resulting in larger execution gaps and emphasising the importance of efficient execution.

Dynamic Slippage adjusts based on market conditions, offering a more adaptable trading experience and enabling swaps to adapt to their environment.

Why should liquidity providers care?

LPs rely on trading activity. When traders enjoy a smoother experience, pools become more appealing, leading to:

✅ Increased swaps  
✅ Higher volume  
✅ Greater fee opportunities  
✅ Improved liquidity utilisation

Enhanced and timely execution benefits both traders and LPs.

Dynamic Slippage is now the default setting on http://edge.meteora.ag. It is also available on the main app, where you can set it as your default option.

For those preferring full control, manual configuration remains accessible via the global settings icon.

Dynamic Slippage represents another step in refining the liquidity experience, streamlining trading while empowering LPs to maximise their capital efficiency.

 2️⃣. Don’t chase every move

3️⃣. Monitor your positions
Markets move fast. Keep an eye on price action, volume, and your range status so you can make better decisions.

4️⃣.  Split your positions
Instead of one big position, consider multiple smaller LP positions. This gives you more flexibility to adjust as the market changes.

5️⃣. Don’t overcommit
Volatility brings opportunity, but risk increases too. Allocate capital with a clear plan.

The goal isn’t to predict every move, It’s to stay positioned, collect fees, and manage risk.

Meteora Series - How dex works (4)
Maximizing Fee Revenue
@meteoraAg @thegoosedao

In the previous posts, we explored lp, swaps, and the fee system. Now I’d like to write about how to maximize fees on DLMM based on this knowledge.

The first thing to keep in mind is that when choosing a token, trading volume is the top priority. 

This seems obvious since LP fees are certain percentage of the swap volume. Therefore, the token you choose should have a decent level of trading volume to back it up. 
Personally, in the current market, I prefer tokens that have around 10M in volume in the main pool on a 24h basis.

Once you’ve chosen a token, the next step is deciding which pool to provide liquidity to. Even for the same token pair, there can be multiple DLMM pools.
When choosing a pool, I always check the two things.

1. Is the pool competitive?

To earn decent amount of fees, the pool needs to have a lot of trading activity. So, when there are multiple pools, it’s advantageous if the pool I’m considering has higher volume.

The reason a pool with higher fees isn’t always the best choice is that Jupiter routes swaps in a way that benefits the swapper. So, all other conditions being equal, a pool with higher fees is less likely to be chosen compared to one with lower fees.

For newly created tokens, volatility is often high enough that 5% fee pool might work for a while. But it quickly gets outcompeted by lower-fee pools, so I don’t particularly favor 5% pools.

Besides fees, the size of the liquidity in the pool (TVL) is also important. If the TVL is too low, the price impact becomes significant, reducing the likelihood of the pool being chosen.

So, you need to look at both the fee percentage and the TVL. By checking the pool in Dexscreener, you can see the trading frequency and get a sense of how active the pool is. So make sure to check it out.

2. Is my liquidity share in the pool appropriate?

Once you’ve chosen a pool, the next thing to consider is to check how much of the pool’s TVL my liquidity will represent.

The fees generated in the pool are shared among liquidity providers. If the pool’s TVL is $100k and I only provide $100, I can’t expect significant fees even if the trading volume is high. So, my liquidity share matters.

When providing liquidity to DLMM, you can see the percentage of your liquidity share for each bin at the bottom. If my goal is to earn fees quickly and exit, I try to keep this share between 15-25% at least.

In extreme cases, if the TVL in all available pools is too high to get a meaningful share, I even consider creating new pool on my own.

From experience, when these two factors align well, I’ve been able to generate high fees in a short amount of time. I’ll share a few successful cases from recently.

Of course It takes some experience to get the hang of this, but knowing these factors from the start will help you learn much faster.
I learned the hard way, figuring things out from scratch.

Summary for today:
1. Trading volume is the top priority.
2. Check the pool competitiveness and your liquidity share.
3. When these factors align, you will earn decent amount of fees.

If you found this post helpful, please repost so that others can see. 🙇‍♂️

Meteora Series - How dex works (3)
@MeteoraAG @thegoosedao

 In the previous posts, I wrote about LP and swap. This time, I’ll dive into the fee structure of dex.

In cex, the exchange takes a certain percentage of the transaction amount, and this is generally accepted. 

However, it’s different in dex. Anyone can create a pool and provide liquidity through smart contracts, And the transaction fees mainly go to the liquidity providers (LP).

Take Uniswap as an example. The v2 model distributes 0.3% of the trading volume to LPs. In v3, when creating a pool, you can choose from 0.01%, 0.05%, 0.3%, or 1%. 

So, does Uniswap not make any money? The Uniswap team only takes a 0.05% fee from a very small number of pools.

Now, let’s talk about Meteora. The fees in DLMM are not fixed. They’ve adopted a structure where fees adjust based on volatility. The higher the volatility, the more risk LPs take, right? Think of the higher fees as a kind of compensation for that risk.

Take a look at this DLMM pool. At first, the multiple values might seem overwhelming, but it’s simpler than it looks.

Base Fee : This is the minimum fee (%) for the pool. It never goes below this value.
Max Fee : Conversely, this is the maximum fee the pool can charge, and it seems DLMM has it fixed at 10%.
Dynamic Fee : This is the fee charged to users when a swap occurs at the current moment. The higher the price volatility, the larger this value becomes.
Protocol Fee : This is the fee the Meteora team takes, which is 5% of the dynamic fee.

Let’s assume the pool’s dynamic fee is 5%. If a user swaps 20 SOL in that pool, they’ll pay 20 * 0.05 = 1 SOL as a fee. 
Of that, 5% of the fee(0.05 SOL) goes to the protocol, and the remaining 0.95 SOL is distributed to the LPs. Since there are usually multiple LPs, the 0.95 SOL is divided among them based on the proportion of liquidity each provided.

Later, as volatility decreases and prices stabilize, the dynamic fee gradually decreases until it eventually matches the base fee. So, the early stages with high price volatility are advantageous for earning higher fees. Of course, the risk is also higher. ☠️

Today’s summary:
1) In dex, the fees paid by swap users mostly go to LPs.
2) DLMM fee dynamically change based on volatility.
3) The higher the volatility, the more fees to LPs.

Meteora Series - How dex works (2)
@MeteoraAG

 In the previous post, I summarized the meaning of providing liquidity in dex. In this post, I’ll cover swap.

If providing liquidity is placing a bid/ask price, swap can be seen as a market order. You’re buying or selling tokens at the price set by the LP provider. In other words, the LP provider is the maker, and the swap user is the taker.

But when you perform a swap, you don’t always get exactly the quote you initially saw on the screen. There are variables that affect the actual number of tokens you receive.

1) price Impact
 This is the percentage that indicates how much the price fluctuates based on the amount of tokens you want to swap. The larger the input, the greater the force moving the price. For example, if some chad swaps 10m USDC for sol at once, the price of SOL would spike. Dex like Jupiter displays the price impact when you swap, so those who pay attention might have noticed.

 If the price jumps with just a bit of buying, we say the liquidity pool is thin. In extreme cases like a new pair pool in https://t.co/Tt1jYIUMQp, even a small purchase can double the price. This is one of the reason why early entry into meme coins is so difficult.

2) slippage
 On the other hand, this is the gap caused by other people’s preceding transactions. If someone buys a large amount before my swap transaction, the price spikes, and I end up swapping for fewer tokens than I originally expected.

 That’s why, when submitting a swap transaction, you set how much slippage you’re willing to tolerate up to a certain percentage. (To be more precise, it’s not a percentage but the minimum number of tokens you’re willing to receive.)

 I bet everyone has experienced a failed transaction due to setting the slippage too low when buying tokens with rapidly changing prices.

so, to the conclusion : 
Swap is the act of executing the market order on the liquidity pool, and the outcome of the swap is affected by the amount of input and the trades of others. 🧐

Meteora Series - How dex works (1)
@MeteoraAG

As I looked through the Meteora posts I wrote, I felt they were kinda fragmented, making it unfriendly for first-time readers. So I’m rewriting them in the desired order, starting from this post.

Let’s go back to the basics: what does it mean to provide LP to a Dex?

When we want to buy or sell something, there needs to be a counterparty. And that counterparty has a desired price. 

That price is called a quote. You’re probably familiar with this kind of screen.

Providing LP is the act of placing these quotes. It’s about offering a price (liquidity) to enable trades to happen.

Let’s assume you are trading on Binance. You placed an order to sell 1 Ethereum for $5k. If the Ethereum price (hopefully) reaches $5k, you’d receive $5k minus fees and order is concluded.

But in a Dex, the concept is a bit different. For example, let’s say I provided liquidity to the sol-usdc pair as below. The bar in the middle shows the current price of the pool, which is $140.86.

What will happen if the price of sol goes up? As the price of sol rises, the sol I provided gets sold, but the proceeds from the sale don’t go directly to my wallet. 

Instead, the usdc paid by the buyer fills the place of the sol. And this usdc becomes part of my liquidity.  
This liquidity then acts as a bid price for sol. In other words, if the price of sol drops, sol will fill that space again.

Naturally, prices don’t move in just one direction. That’s why, as trades happen, the quantity of the two tokens in the liquidity keeps changing, as the state of the pool shifts.

Below is the state of the pool about an hour later, and you can already see a change in the token amounts I initially provided. As the price of sol went up, the amount of sol in the pool decreased, and the amount of usdc increased. From a trading perspective, sol was sold.

This process continues to repeat until the LP provider withdraws their liquidity. In the pool above, if the price of sol exceeds $142.78, all the sol in my liquidity will be sold, leaving only usdc. If I withdraw at that point, I’d receive only usdc (+ LP fees). Conversely, if the price of sol drops below $138.95, my pool would be left with only sol.  

To sum it up, the liquidity in a dex acts as both a bid price and an ask price depending on the current price.

I think understanding this concept will help you know what you’re really doing when you provide LP.

What is a Single Sided Position?

A single sided position means your liquidity begins on one side of the price range rather than being split between two tokens. This allows you to provide liquidity using only one token.

Example:
You deposit only $SOL into a $SOL- $GLUE pool.

Your aim is to let the position convert as the price fluctuates while earning fees from trading activity.  

You can place your liquidity on one side of the price range and collect fees while your strategy unfolds.

✅Select the token you want to provide.

✅Select your volatility strategy

✅Place your liquidity on one side of the current price.

✅Deposit and monitor your position.

It can be useful when you have a market view.

Provide only the token side and let your LP position work as price moves.

[1] @Jaypee_Sol
LPing During Volatility is profitable but also super risky if not properly executed.

Volatile markets can be a nightmare but they also create opportunities for those who manage positions properly.

A few things to keep in mind  when LPing @MeteoraAG during intense volatility. 👇

1️⃣. Use wider ranges
High volatility can push tight ranges inactive quickly. Wider ranges help your liquidity stay active through larger price moves.

 2️⃣. Don’t chase every move
Constantly moving ranges after every pump or dump can hurt your strategy and increase unnecessary IL.

3️⃣. Monitor your positions
Markets move fast. Keep an eye on price action, volume, and your range status so you can make better decisions.

4️⃣.  Split your positions
Instead of one big position, consider multiple smaller LP positions. This gives you more flexibility to adjust as the market changes.

5️⃣. Don’t overcommit
Volatility brings opportunity, but risk increases too. Allocate capital with a clear plan.

The goal isn’t to predict every move, It’s to stay positioned, collect fees, and manage risk.

Volatility rewards LPs who manage risk, not those who react emotionally.

Find your range.Manage your position.
Collect fees.

[1] @MeteoraFR
Comment éviter 90% des rugs et scams avant de LP sur un token 🧵

5 checks rapides à faire à chaque fois (screenshots à l'appui) 👇 https://t.co/EvfPKxwR8t

[2] @MeteoraFR
Le VOLUME 📊

Il n'est pas rare de voir un token à 30k-50k par minute. C'est plutôt rare en ce moment mais ça peut arriver. 

-> Est-ce que le ratio volume / liquidité tient de bout ? 
-> Est-ce que le volume est organique càd réel, réalisé par des vrais traders ? 

Un vrai token n'aura pas de bougies uniformes, il aura des moments un peu creux, des heures avec moins de volume puis d'autres avec beaucoup de volume d'un coup. L'explication est souvent un KOL qui achète, un shill sur X, Toly qui a mentionné le token etc

[3] @MeteoraFR
Les TOP HOLDERS 🐋

On ouvre la répartition et on check le top 10 voire même le top 100. 

🚩 Quelques wallets qui tiennent 30-40%+ (hors LP, burn, CEX) → potentiel énorme dump.

Ce qu'on veut : 
-> une distribution saine. Pas 3 wallets capables de tuer la chart en un clic.
-> des wallets de traders déjà actifs sur solana, grâce à @frontrunpro vous pouvez avoir l'historique des wallets et voir en un coup d'oeil si ce wallet hold d'autres tokens ou a un historique de haut PNL sur un certain memecoin etc..

[4] @MeteoraFR
Les TOTAL FEES 💸

Les fees générées te disent si l'activité est réelle.
Beaucoup de fees = du vrai trading, du vrai intérêt.
Des fees ridicules sur un token soi-disant avec énormément de volume → c'est du fake volume pour t'attirer. 

Règle à retenir : un token avec 100k de marketcap devrait avoir minimum 10 SOL de total fees, 200k -> 20 SOL de fees, 300k -> 30 SOL etc

[5] @MeteoraFR
La BUBBLEMAP 🫧

Le check le plus sous-coté. Elle montre les CONNEXIONS entre wallets mais également la valeur du cluster en dollars. 

🚩 Top holders tous reliés ou financés par la même source → une seule personne contrôle le supply. Rug programmé. 

Parfois, comme dans le screenshot ci-dessous, les bubbles ne sont pas connectées entre elles mais elles sont quand même suspectes, elle n'ont même pas fait un trade, ni un seul envoi sur un cold wallet ni rien, suspect comme jamais. 

L'icône verte montre que le wallet vient juste d'être créé, pas bon.

[6] @MeteoraFR
Les VIEWERS de la chart 👀

L'outil le plus sous-côté de tous. Il s'agit du nombre de gens qui regardent en live = le niveau d'attention réel.

Beaucoup de viewers + volume organique = momentum sain.
Peu de viewers mais « volume » élevé = scam.

Selon mon expérience le nombre de viewers est toujours plus élevé sur @gmgnai plutôt que sur @TradingTerminal. 

200 viewers sur terminal et 600 sur GMGN = OK 👌

[7] @MeteoraFR
Conclusion

En résumé, avant de LP je vérifie :

✅ Volume
✅ Top holders
✅ Total fees
✅ Bubblemap
✅ Viewers

5 min qui t'évitent de te faire instant rug (et à force ça te prendra juste un coup d'oeil)

NFA, fais toujours tes propres recherches BIEN SÛR. 

Si l'analyse t'a plu et que t'en veux d'autres partage le thread pour nous donner d'la force et surtout DON'T GET RUGGED ☄️ https://t.co/lh48A8K96g
